agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle class enforcement, and configurable stop duration. This is a core operational feature that spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, fleet/driver management, delivery lifecycle, and customer communication.",
    "bounded_context": "This feature spans at least four bounded contexts: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization algorithms, recalculation triggers, and stop time estimation; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle class licensing, zone assignments, and shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-transit, failed, completed), zone classification, and package attributes including size/weight class; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery proximity windows, and customer communication preferences. The spec conflates these contexts, which is a risk for domain drift.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in and ending when they clock out or complete all assigned deliveries. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize total travel time/distance within a driver's assigned zone, respecting vehicle class constraints." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic region used to partition deliveries and assign them to drivers. A delivery belongs to exactly one zone. A driver is assigned to one or more zones per shift." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been accepted into the system but not yet assigned to a route or not yet attempted. Only pending deliveries are eligible for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "An event that invalidates the current optimized route and requires the system to recompute it. Three defined triggers: new delivery in zone, delivery marked failed, driver deviation." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A rolling proximity window defined as the next three delivery stops on the driver's active route, used to trigger customer notifications." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of what a driver's vehicle can legally and physically carry (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determined by the vehicle assigned to the driver for their shift, not the driver alone." },
      { "term": "Delivery Vehicle Class Requirement", "definition": "The minimum vehicle class required to transport a given delivery, determined by package dimensions, weight, or regulatory category." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide but can be overridden per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Driver Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the suggested optimized route beyond a defined threshold." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (e.g., recipient unavailable, address inaccessible). Returns the delivery to a re-assignable or terminal state." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery whose vehicle class requirement exceeds their licensed vehicle class for the current shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Violating this is a physical impossibility (motorcycle cannot carry freight) and likely a legal/regulatory violation. If this rule is broken, the system is in a corrupt state — the delivery is undeliverable and may create liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D on a driver's route, D.requiredVehicleClass <= driver.currentShift.vehicleClass"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When a driver starts a shift, the system MUST automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in their assigned zone(s) that are compatible with their vehicle class.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy — the business could decide to allow manual route creation or deferred assignment. The automation trigger is a business decision, not a physical constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Within T seconds of shift start, a route exists for the driver containing only pending, zone-matched, vehicle-class-compatible deliveries"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's active zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an optimization policy. The business could decide to batch recalculations or defer them. The system would still function (just sub-optimally) without immediate recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an active route for driver D in zone Z, when a new delivery is created in zone Z, a recalculation event is triggered and the route is updated to include the new delivery if vehicle-class-compatible"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. A stale route with a failed stop is inefficient but not corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an active route containing delivery D, when D is marked failed, the route is recalculated excluding D from the sequence"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. The driver can still complete deliveries on a stale route.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver deviation is detected (position or sequence divergence beyond threshold), a recalculation is triggered using the driver's current position as the new origin"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a customer experience policy. Failure to notify does not corrupt data or violate physical constraints, though it degrades service quality.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery moves into position 1, 2, or 3 on the active route (and was not previously in those positions), exactly one notification is sent to the associated customer"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Configurable operational parameter. A VP could change this tomorrow.",
      "testable_assertion": "When no zone-level override exists, route time calculations use 240 seconds per stop"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The operations team MAY override the stop duration per zone, and zone-level overrides take precedence over the system default.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Configurable operational parameter with a precedence rule.",
      "testable_assertion": "When zone Z has a stop duration override of N minutes, all route calculations for zone Z use N minutes per stop instead of the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A delivery MUST belong to exactly one zone at any point in time.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A delivery in multiple zones simultaneously would cause duplicate assignment, double-delivery attempts, and data corruption. Zone assignment is a partitioning constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D in the system, COUNT(zones containing D) == 1"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "Only deliveries in 'pending' status are eligible for route assignment.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Assigning a completed, failed, or in-transit delivery to a new route would corrupt delivery state and potentially cause duplicate delivery attempts. This is a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D being added to a route, D.status == 'pending'"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 5 pending deliveries in their zone compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "An optimized route containing all 5 deliveries is generated and assigned to the driver within the defined SLA"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to their zone that is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery in an optimized position, resulting in 9 stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and delivery #4 is marked as failed",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The route is recalculated excluding the failed delivery; remaining stops are re-optimized from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with stops [A, B, C, D, E] and skips B to go directly to C",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position, re-incorporating B (if still pending) in an optimized position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver's active route has stops [A, B, C, D, E] where C is a customer's delivery",
      "when": "The driver completes stop A (making B, C, D the next 3 stops)",
      "then": "Customer C receives exactly one notification that their delivery is within the next 3 stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A motorcycle driver is assigned to a zone containing a freight shipment and 3 parcel deliveries",
      "when": "The route is generated at shift start",
      "then": "The route contains only the 3 parcel deliveries; the freight shipment is NOT included and remains unassigned to this driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "The operations team has set zone 'Downtown' stop duration to 6 minutes",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in the Downtown zone",
      "then": "Each stop's estimated duration uses 6 minutes, not the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver's route is recalculated due to a new delivery being added",
      "when": "The recalculation changes the next-3-stops window",
      "then": "Customers newly entering the window are notified; customers who were already notified are NOT notified again"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are zero pending deliveries in their zone",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "The driver is assigned an empty route (or placed in a waiting state), and when a new delivery enters their zone, a route is generated"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "Two drivers are active in the same zone",
      "when": "A new pending delivery enters the zone",
      "then": "The delivery is assigned to exactly one driver's route (not both), based on optimization criteria"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A new delivery is added to the zone while a route recalculation is already in progress", "expected_behavior": "The system must either queue the trigger and recalculate again after the current one completes, or incorporate the new delivery into the in-progress calculation. Two concurrent recalculations must not produce conflicting routes." },
    { "scenario": "A driver deviates from the route but then returns to the original sequence", "expected_behavior": "The deviation detection must have a threshold or cooldown to avoid unnecessary recalculation churn. A brief GPS drift should not trigger recalculation." },
    { "scenario": "All deliveries on a driver's route are marked as failed", "expected_behavior": "The driver should end up with an empty route and return to a waiting/idle state, not be stuck with a route containing zero stops." },
    { "scenario": "A customer's delivery is at position 4, enters the next-3 window, gets pushed back to position 5 by a recalculation, then re-enters the window", "expected_behavior": "The customer should receive at most one notification per route lifecycle to avoid notification spam. The spec must define: is this 'once ever' or 'once per window entry'?" },
    { "scenario": "A delivery enters the zone but no active driver has a compatible vehicle class", "expected_behavior": "The delivery must remain in pending state and not be force-assigned to an incompatible driver. The system should surface this as an operational alert." },
    { "scenario": "The operations team changes the stop duration override for a zone while drivers are mid-shift", "expected_behavior": "The spec does not define whether active routes should be recalculated with the new duration or only apply to future routes. This needs clarification." },
    { "scenario": "A driver is assigned to multiple overlapping zones", "expected_behavior": "The route optimization must consider deliveries from all assigned zones and produce a single unified route, not separate per-zone routes." },
    { "scenario": "A recalculation trigger fires but the route optimizer produces the same sequence", "expected_behavior": "The system should detect no-change scenarios and avoid sending redundant notifications or route updates to the driver's device." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's shift ends while deliveries are still on the route", "expected_behavior": "Undelivered stops must return to pending status and be eligible for reassignment. Customer notifications for those stops may need to be rescinded or updated." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid-fire recalculation triggers: 10 new deliveries added within 30 seconds", "expected_behavior": "The system should debounce or batch recalculation triggers to avoid thrashing the optimizer. The spec does not define any throttling behavior." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries-per-shift (driver utilization), and reduces failed delivery retry costs. A well-optimized route can improve delivery throughput by 15-30%. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly failed attempts and potential regulatory fines. Configurable stop duration allows per-zone cost tuning.",
    "operational": "This feature introduces significant operational complexity: real-time route recalculation requires low-latency compute, GPS tracking infrastructure, and robust event processing. The operations team gains a new control surface (stop duration overrides) that requires tooling and training. Recalculation triggers create system load that must be managed (debouncing, queuing).",
    "user": "Drivers get clear route guidance, reducing cognitive load and decision fatigue. Customers get proactive delivery proximity notifications, improving satisfaction and reducing 'where is my package' support calls. However, frequent route changes may frustrate drivers who prefer predictability.",
    "risk": "High risk areas: (1) Recalculation race conditions could cause delivery double-assignment across drivers; (2) Vehicle class enforcement failure has legal/safety implications; (3) Notification spam from recalculation churn degrades customer trust; (4) The spec is silent on multi-driver zone coordination, which is the most complex operational scenario.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary workflow change — shifts from manual route planning to system-guided delivery. Gain efficiency but lose autonomy. Deviation detection may feel like surveillance." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Receive new proximity notifications improving visibility. Risk of notification spam if recalculation edge cases are not handled." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gain new zone-level configuration capability (stop duration). Need tooling to manage overrides. Must monitor for unassignable deliveries (vehicle class mismatches)." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Not mentioned in spec but likely affected — automated route assignment may reduce or change their role. Need clarity on manual override capabilities." },
      { "group": "Engineering/Platform", "impact": "Must build real-time event processing, GPS deviation detection, route optimization engine, and notification pipeline. Significant infrastructure investment." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 55,
    "reasoning": "The spec covers the happy path well but leaves critical operational scenarios undefined: multi-driver coordination within a zone, recalculation concurrency/debouncing, deviation detection thresholds, notification deduplication rules, shift-end behavior, and mid-shift configuration changes. The vehicle class enforcement is clear. The bounded context boundaries are implicit and could lead to coupling if not explicitly drawn. Several edge cases require product decisions before implementation.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class enforcement is a hard invariant — clearly defined and unambiguous",
      "Recalculation triggers are well-enumerated (3 specific events)",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is clear",
      "Next-3-stops notification trigger is well-defined for the happy path"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver zone coordination — how are deliveries partitioned between drivers in the same zone?",
      "Deviation detection threshold — what constitutes a 'deviation'? GPS drift vs intentional reroute?",
      "Notification deduplication — should customers be re-notified if they leave and re-enter the 3-stop window?",
      "Recalculation concurrency — what happens when triggers overlap?",
      "Shift lifecycle — what happens to assigned deliveries when a shift ends?",
      "Dispatcher role — can dispatchers manually override automated routes?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver is assigned to one or more zones at shift start and this does not change mid-shift",
      "Vehicle class is determined by the vehicle assigned to the driver for the shift, not a driver-level attribute",
      "Route optimization means minimizing total travel time or distance (the actual objective function is unspecified)",
      "The 'next 3 stops' window is based on route sequence position, not geographic proximity or time",
      "A delivery that fails is removed from the route entirely (not re-queued on the same route)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Multi-driver zone coordination is completely unspecified. When two drivers are active in the same zone and a new delivery arrives, the spec says 'recalculate' but does not define how deliveries are partitioned between drivers. This is the single most complex aspect of the feature and cannot be deferred.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Dispatchers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Each driver 'owns' a subset of deliveries once assigned; new deliveries go to the driver with the most capacity",
        "B: All deliveries in a zone are re-optimized across all active drivers on every trigger (full re-partition)",
        "C: A dispatcher manually assigns new deliveries to a specific driver"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A for v1 — assign new deliveries to the driver with the lightest load or nearest proximity. Full re-partition (B) is operationally better but significantly more complex and will cause route instability for drivers."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Deviation detection threshold is undefined. 'Driver manually deviates from the suggested route' could mean: skipping a stop, taking a different road to the same stop, or GPS position being X meters off the planned path. Each interpretation triggers different recalculation behavior and has different false-positive rates.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Engineering/Platform"],
      "options": [
        "A: Deviation = driver completes a stop out of sequence (sequence-based detection)",
        "B: Deviation = driver's GPS is more than N meters from the planned path for more than M seconds (position-based detection)",
        "C: Both A and B"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A for v1 — sequence-based detection is simpler, deterministic, and avoids GPS noise issues. Position-based detection can be added later."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Customer notification deduplication policy is not defined. When route recalculations cause a delivery to oscillate in and out of the next-3-stops window, the spec does not say whether the customer should be re-notified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Notify once per delivery, ever (first entry into the window)",
        "B: Notify once per route calculation (could receive multiple if recalculations move them in and out)",
        "C: Notify once, with an update notification if their position changes significantly"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — notify exactly once when the delivery first enters the 3-stop window. Additional notifications create spam and erode customer trust."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Shift lifecycle and end-of-shift behavior is not addressed. What happens to deliveries still on a driver's route when their shift ends? Are they automatically unassigned and returned to pending? Is there a handoff process? Can a shift be extended?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Undelivered stops automatically return to pending at shift end",
        "B: Shift cannot end until all assigned deliveries are completed or failed",
        "C: Operations team manually reassigns remaining deliveries"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A with an operational alert — forcing shift completion (B) has labor law implications. Automatic return to pending ensures no deliveries are lost."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The optimization objective function is not defined. 'Optimized route' could mean: minimize total distance, minimize total time, minimize latest delivery time, maximize deliveries per hour, or balance fairness across drivers. The choice fundamentally changes route behavior.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Minimize total travel time (most common)",
        "B: Minimize total distance",
        "C: Minimize maximum customer wait time",
        "D: Configurable per zone by operations team"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A (minimize total travel time) as default, with Option D as a future enhancement. Time-based optimization accounts for traffic and road conditions better than pure distance."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
